speaker: interviewer
text: Quiet on the stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is slate. Henry Aizenman. Robert Cooper is the interviewer. This is tape number four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry, we were talking about the last camps you were in, Ravensbruck and some of these others. How were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first thing we saw-- of course there were rumors-- but the first thing I saw was the guards starting to get in their trucks and some of them run away by foot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as-- as that happened, we broke into the armory and helped ourselves to some of their rifles and started chasing them and shooting at them, and they were running away. Some of the prisoners-- I myself didn't know how to work a rifle, but I learned quickly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 14. This was on May 2, 1945. You know, I-- I have a-- what I call a born-again birthday party every year on May 2 or close to it, as long-- the closest Sunday to it. And I have a great big born-again birthday party. I invite all my friends here in town. Not-- not necessarily survivors of concentration camp. Those people are too dour for my taste. And we just celebrate with food, drink, and music.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the guards killed by the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This isn't-- this isn't something that I particularly like to talk about, but quite a few guards were killed, and some of the prisoners were killed, too, because the guards were shooting back. And then we saw a Jeep with American soldiers with an American flag ride down the road. And when they saw us, we looked-- we looked like a bunch of hooligan terrorists. They just turned. We started waving at them and running towards them, and they just turned the Jeep around and took off, which was just as well. They may have been overwhelmed by our kindness.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were free. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For some reason my buddy and I separated, and I latched onto two other guys, and we started going around in the neighborhood and eating. As a matter of fact, before that happened, my buddy and I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now imagine this. The Americans liberated us. They took us out of the hellhole. He and I organized. We stole an American truck full of C-rations. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we managed to get it into the woods, dig a hole, and we buried the C-rations for some future time. Maybe this was just a joke. And we're going to be short of food. We actually did that. And then-- and then I overate and got sick, and I wound up in the hospital for just a couple of days. And also I started-- I went over to the other zone, to the Russian zone, and I found out that there were other people besides American soldiers. There were Russian soldiers over there also. But everything was very friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What physical condition were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible physical condition, but improving very quickly. And I was a 14-year-old, and nothing fazes a 14-year-old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how much you weighed at--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: The time of liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I didn't know there was such a thing as a scale to weigh human beings. And then I-- I was really kicking up my heels. Like, for instance, I acquired a-- a gun from a German officer. I acquired a motorcycle-- either that, or I think I traded a gun for a motorcycle, or the other way around. I ran out of gas, and there was nothing I could do about running a motorcycle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I started traveling around Europe, some guys and I. Some guys said, why don't we go to-- to Rome? Just as a lark?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing else to do. And besides, we hadn't imagined. I was-- I was one of the most undereducated people you have ever seen in your life. I was 14 years old, and the only schools I had been to besides first and second grade were the schools of hard knocks-- which I learned a lot, but nothing-- not subjects that a 14-year-old should learn.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we hopped the train and went to Rome. And this is a really funny story, because in Rome there was a rumor-- now, in those days I had acquired some other clothing besides my striped pants and jacket-- there was a rumor in Rome that the Vatican was giving out money. And the Italians made great ice cream.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now in Germany, everything was lawless. You wanted something, you went and-- and got it. Like, if-- if you wanted a chicken, you went to a farm and if you hunted-- I-- I used to try and shoot with my-- with my little bitty gun, I tried to shoot chickens. I never hit anyone. But if you grabbed-- if you got a hold of a chicken, you wrung its neck, and you cooked it. And you had chicken.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in Italy there were-- there was law and order, more or less. And there was a rumor that in the Vatican they were giving out money. There was only one thing-- you had to convert. So I said, well, for an ice cream, why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were giving everybody 10,000 or 15,000 lire, which was the equivalent, I think, of a quarter, but you could get a great big ice cream cone for that. So I went and converted, and I got me an ice cream. And it was the first ice cream I tasted in five years. And then it tasted so good I wanted to go convert again. They cut down on the-- on the amount of money. They only gave you 10,000 lire. But we found a vendor-- I converted under a different name-- we found a vendor who was selling ice cream for 10,000 lire. Anyhow, I found out things. You can't convert every day of your life. So I-- I went back to Germany, and then I decided I wanted to go home to Poland to see if anybody survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: First of all, why did you return to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because from Italy where else can you go? I mean, you could go into-- into the ocean. I-- I had no business in France, in Switzerland. They wouldn't would let me in. The-- the only place where they would let you in easily would be-- was into Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went back to Germany, and then I hopped-- I found out which trains were going in my direction, which is Poland. As a matter of fact, I hopped a train that had all kinds of factory equipment that the Russians were taking back from Germany to Russia. And I got off in-- in Poland someplace, and then I hopped another train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, it's curious that at the time I had acquired, through one way or another, civilian clothes, and my hair had grown up. By the way, through all this time, my-- I had been-- I had a-- a sort of a really bald haircut, and my hair had started growing back. And when I came to Poland, I-- we found-- I found a place where they were givi-- where they would give you a meal, but I didn't know if they would give meals to Jewish people. So [SIGH] I became a non-Jewish person.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what's your name? And I gave him some kind of a Polish-sounding name, and I got a meal. But to show you that even after the war I was afraid to appear Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went back to Poland, and I found out that Przytyk didn't exist anymore. But the town next to it, which was about 10 miles away, Radom, which was a substantial town--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-A-D-O-M. Radom was the closest town to it. And I finally found an aunt of mine-- my Aunt Bela, my mother's younger sister. She had been so-- sort of mutilated during the war. She had lost an eye and several toes. She was in bad shape, but at least I found somebody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at the time the Russians were all over Poland, and Pol-- Poland had a communist government. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I tried to get some of our stuff-- some of our things back from some farmers, those farmer friends of ours near Przytyk. And the first thing they say when they see me and I say I am-- I am Henry Aizenman, the small Aizenman, they look at me like I was some kind of an-- a wild animal. They said [NON-ENGLISH], which means little Aizenman, you're still alive-- with surprise. And then they start telling me that the Germans took everything away that we had left with them. Well, the way they were looking at me, they were looking at me like-- like you look at a turkey around Thanksgiving. I-- I got out of there in a hurry. And I said, there's nothing to be gained from this, and I went back to Radom. And after several weeks in Radom-- that was already the end of 1945, towards the end of 1945. And an incident occurred in Radom. Some friends of ours who had also survived concentration camp, they were tailors, and they had befriended a Jewish major who was a physician-- a doctor. And he would come over to their house for dinner occasionally. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one day the Polish Underground-- which was anti-Russian, anti-communist-- one day the Polish Underground cut all their throats while they were sitting at dinner. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's when we decided-- my aunt and I, and she had met this man whom she was going to marry-- we decided that it was time to leave Poland, never to return again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we left Poland and went to-- on our way to Germany, back to Germany, since we'd heard of displaced persons camps and also emigration policies. We wanted to emigrate someplace. And we-- we took trains. We went through Czechoslovakia and Austria, and we went back to Germany. I'd like to interject. The only people-- the only East Europeans who treated me in a humane way and in a nice way, like a human being, were the Czech people. And it's not something that you can forget. Can you remember any specific acts of kindness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Specific acts of kindness. We-- we went through Czechoslovakia. They fed us. I mean, not only one person, but several instances.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is there anything you need? Do you need an overcoat? We will direct you where you can go to-- to cross the border into Germany. Is there anything else we can do for you? Things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were acts of kindness in general. And I don't know if it has anything to do with nationality or upbringing, or if we just ran into a nice bunch of people. And then we went back to Germany and-- with my aunt and uncle, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we went to a displaced persons camp. The-- they had established some displaced persons camps, and we went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was near Frankfurt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I don't remem-- I think it was called Zeilsheim-- Z-E-I-L-S-H-E-I-M. Zeilsheim. And I stayed there several months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I-- I was-- I was taking Hebrew lessons. Now that was in 1946, and they were talking about establishing-- about emigrating to Palestine, as it was called at the time. And I was taking Hebrew lessons, and I was trying to catch up on my reading. I was always with a book. As a matter of fact, there was a movie about four miles away. We used to walk to the movie. I would walk with a book in my hand while I was walking for-- for miles on end, and I would read. It-- it was a great enjoyment. I-- I've never lost my enthusiasm for reading.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what sorts of things you were reading?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I was reading westerns written by a-- by a German author. His name was Karl May. But I would read just about anything. I didn't care. I would read Goethe as well as Karl May.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of movies were being shown?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were showing-- they were showing mostly American movies. Some German movies, but they weren't showing very many German movies because they had too much propaga-- Nazi propaganda.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember I saw a movie in Germany. It was called which means And then I found out that the American name of that movie was but what if-- if-- starring Joel McCrea, Barbara Stanwyck, and Robert Preston. It was my first American movie that I had seen after the war. But when you translate it, what would the Germans know about Union Pacific?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, that is when I started liking people, and I-- I have never lost my liking for people. Some-- some of my friends say, how in the world can you be so good-natured and like people after what you've been through? It's-- I don't know. You decide that-- how you want to live your life. Do you want to be a bitter old guy, or do you want to join society and be a-- a real person? And I hope I've gotten to be a real person.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when I was living with my aunt and uncle, they-- my aunt gave birth to a daughter in 1946. Named her Rochelle after her-- after my aunt's mother. I found out that I had another aunt, an aunt whom I had seen in-- in Auschwitz. Well, she-- well, that-- that old hag was beating up on her. And they lived in another part of Germany, in southern Germany, called Garmisch-Partenkirchen-- and don't ask me to spell it. It's in the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could-- Can you tell me your aunt's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My aunt that I had been living with was Bela, and her last name was the same as my mother's-- Zajde, Z-A-J-D-E. And she married a man by the name of Jacob Miller. And Jacob Miller was also from Przytyk, by the way. As a matter of fact, he had lost his whole family. I-- I remember that I was going to Hebrew school when I was six, seven years old. I was attending Hebrew school with Jack Miller's kids. One of them was even older than I was. So what goes around comes around. Anyhow, I found out that my aunt-- that her name was Frieda. Now, her name was the same as my father. She's from my father's side. Frieda Aizenman. And she had married a man by the name of Maryan Tamboryn-- T-A-M-B-O-R-Y-N. And I liked-- they were much more liberal than my uncle Jack Miller and Bela. They had a different kind of lifestyle, and I liked it better over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by liberal?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could do what I wanted to. I didn't have to go to Hebrew school every morning. I didn't have to go to prayers every Friday night and every Saturday. Also, the food was better, and the scenery was much better. They didn't-- they lived in a private house. They did not live in a displaced persons camp. And I liked it much better over there, so I-- so I stayed there. And I stayed with them for about two years, occasionally coming visiting my other aunt and uncle. And over there I was trying to catch up on my education. I had never learned how to swim. I-- I learned how to swim. I had-- I hadn't been to school in years and years, and for about six months I went to German school-- except I was deficient in many subjects, and everybody was looking at me as if I were a freak. So I freaked out and told them I don't want to go to this school. So we even had a private tutor who would come in and teach several of us. And I really enjoyed being in Garmisch at the time, but at the same time, you were living among your torturers, and I wanted to emigrate. To me, it didn't make much difference. I-- at the time, I was very-- a patriotic Zionist. Maybe I caught it from my father and mother. But I wanted to go to Palestine, which was illegal at the time. And I had already signed up to-- to get on a boat to be smuggled into Italy and from there go to Palestine, and then I got a better offer. Somebody told me I could emigrate to the United States, which was actually the country I always wanted to go to.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because everybody knows that the streets are paved with gold. [LAUGHS] And I mean, it's obvious. There was nobody in Europe that I knew of, except maybe a few exceptions, who did not want to go to the United States, who did not want to go to America, as it was called.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the opportunity?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I signed up. And as a matter of fact, a friend of mine-- Allen, whom I met in Garmisch-- he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he had also survived concentration camp, and he signed up. And he says, hey, Henry. You want to sign up to go to America? I say, all right. I'll go with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we hopped a train, went to Munich, and signed up. As a matter of fact, I was called before he was, and they-- they were going to send me. And I said, I'm not going without Allen. So eventually in January 1948, after a stay in a children's camp-- which was actually supervised by the-- I think it was United States Committee for Care of European Children. It was a United Nations agency headed by Mrs. Eleanor Roosevelt. So I have Mrs. Roosevelt to thank for my being here, and the UN. And where did you come?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we arrived in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you there long?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there several weeks, and it was a lot of fun. As a matter of fact, there was a blizzard in New York at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed at a-- a-- a sort of a charity children's home. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at-- at the time I was beginning to learn some English, like, and I wanted-- I wanted some American food. Didn't have much money. I did have two granduncles. My grandmoth-- my grandmother had two brothers who were up in-- in-- in their 60s who lived in New York. And they want-- actually wanted me to stay with them, but I decided no. I wanted to see the country, and I wanted to see the world. And I didn't want to interrupt their lives. And at the time I also realized that people-- Jewish people in this country-- really didn't know what had been going on in Europe. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was once among a group of them, and we-- we were having dinner or something, and they started asking me questions. And I actually-- I actually started telling them the way things really were, and they looked at me as if I was telling them stories that I had just made up on the spur of the moment. That's when I decided--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel-- how did you feel about that reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: It did-- it didn't bother me. I was indignant, but I kept quiet about it. I learned to mask my feelings at the time. And it's understandable that-- that they didn't believe me, because who could believe anything like that? And that's when I decided I was going to keep my mouth shut until necessary. And when somebody asked me, well, how-- everybody wanted to know-- well, how were things over there in concentration camp, I'd say oh, pretty bad, and that's all-- without details.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in New York several weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I was in New York, I think, several months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Several months.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I went to a-- I went to one of those New York cafeterias where they make sandwiches to order. And my English wasn't that good, but I wasn't bashful about it. Nobody else in New York spoke good English. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I see-- the guy in front of me says, I'll have a sandwich. It sounded good to me. I say, I'll have a son of a bitch too.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] That was one of my amusing experiences in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then I started to go-- I-- I discovered the movies. I probably hold the world's record. I had-- my uncles had given me some money-- not much, but some-- my granduncles. And I went out on 42nd Street. In those days, it was still safe. I went to 42nd Street, and I went into one movie where I would see a double feature that lasted something like three hours. I'd have a slice of pizza for a dime, go into the next movie which-- that showed a double feature. I once spelt-- spent as much as 12 hours going from one movie to another. I think it helped me with my English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And after that, they want-- they wanted to send us to certain places that had social agencies that would help us. But my friend and I-- Allen, whom I had met in-- in Garmisch-- we decided that we wanted to go to a place where there were cowboys. And we decided the best place to go was Kansas City, since St. Louis was too big a city and we assumed-- Kansas City was called the Heart of America. It was a-- right smack in the central part of the United States, and we decided we wanted to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after a stayover of several weeks in Cleveland at a-- at a-- at an orphanage, where I started learning some things academically-- there were-- I remember there was-- a professor from one of the colleges came in and started-- and started giving us math lessons. I wish I remembered some of the tricks he taught us.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we went to Kansas City, and in Kansas City the social agencies helped us. They placed us with another-- with a family whose name was Tager-- T-A-G-E-R-- who had come from Germany in the early '30s, and we stayed with them. They paid them a stipend. And we went to high school, which wasn't-- which wasn't very easy, since my English wasn't up to par and also my knowledge was lacking. I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year are we in now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're-- we're talking about 1948. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I remember the-- this high school. It was Central High School. Was about two, three miles away from where we lived. And the bus ride cost a dime, but we wanted to save money, so we would-- we would just walk and run to school to save a dime. In those days, you could get things for a dime. And then went to school over there for about six, seven months, and then I decided I got a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a job-- as a matter of fact, in Germany while I was living with my aunt and uncle, my uncle had opened a fur store. He was-- he had been a furrier before the war. So I learned some of this, so I went to work as a fur nailer in Kansas City for the Alaskan Fur Company. I think I was making $27 a week, which was good money in those days. And then I decided I needed more education, so I-- I started going to junior college nights. I learned some typing, and I learned some other things. And all this time, I-- I always read a lot. I'm a prolific reader. In Kansas City, I-- I discovered comic magazines. I was 18 year-- 18 years old, and I-- and I was reading all the comics I could get a hold of. And then several of survivors, we got together-- five of us-- and we rented an apartment, and we were doing our own cooking. And they were always making fun of me because I was always reading-- comic books, books, you name it. I started in one library. I would read my way through the stacks. I started with westerns and-- and then started with mysteries.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's good. OK. I think--
